title: Take WSL path from PATH instead of forcing it to WindowsApps
body:
<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

None

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Podman on Windows now prefers the WSL executable in “C:\Program Files\Windows Subsystem for Linux\wsl.exe” over the one in WindowsApps, avoiding common “access denied” issues.

```

According to issue https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25787 podman is trying to use WindowsApps wsl.exe first, but that one can have access issues

We can first search on Program Files for the proper .exe for wsl, this corrects issues with WindowsApps folder executables not being accessible

comments.body: [NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.

This was added intentionally in https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/4fffa78eec4ec7ab7381b1c7ad9d5c2bd315ac97 because want to us the app store version over the other one?
How do we know that this is no longer needed? And if this is no longer needed would not be the correct thing to revert that commit and just use "wsl" and let %PATH% resolve it?

> This was added intentionally in [4fffa78](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/4fffa78eec4ec7ab7381b1c7ad9d5c2bd315ac97) because want to us the app store version over the other one? How do we know that this is no longer needed? And if this is no longer needed would not be the correct thing to revert that commit and just use "wsl" and let %PATH% resolve it?

i saw a few errors happening to me and others while trying to install podman where it was attempting to access the wsl.exe file in WindowsApps but not being able to. 

I had to delete wsl.exe from my WindowsApps to be able to install it, so I thought this would make sense, but if there is a reason to keep it that way no worries for me.

> This was added intentionally in [4fffa78](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/4fffa78eec4ec7ab7381b1c7ad9d5c2bd315ac97) because want to us the app store version over the other one? How do we know that this is no longer needed? And if this is no longer needed would not be the correct thing to revert that commit and just use "wsl" and let %PATH% resolve it?

@Luap99, [4fffa78](https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/4fffa78eec4ec7ab7381b1c7ad9d5c2bd315ac97) doesn't seem to be linked to an issue, so it's hard to tell what the reason was to prefer the `WindowsApps`. @craigloewen-msft [is suggesting](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25787#issuecomment-2840272846) to change it.

The commit was added in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/21666 which links to an error from https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/21645 so there is some history.

Now I guess as first step we can see if CI passes on this. But as general thing (and I have zero clue about how these different wsl binaries behave) what CI cannot cover is the "podman update" scenario. If we use the Appdata version today and now switch update to the Program Files version will it still see/use all the same WSL instances that have been created previously? If it does then I guess there is little risk to switching this.

And overall if windows is supposed to figure out the right location based on %PATH% then I think it makes more sense to just let it do that and not hard code another full path instead

> The commit was added in #21666 which links to an error from #21645 so there is some history.
> 
> Now I guess as first step we can see if CI passes on this. But as general thing (and I have zero clue about how these different wsl binaries behave) what CI cannot cover is the "podman update" scenario. If we use the Appdata version today and now switch update to the Program Files version will it still see/use all the same WSL instances that have been created previously? If it does then I guess there is little risk to switching this.
> 
> And overall if windows is supposed to figure out the right location based on %PATH% then I think it makes more sense to just let it do that and not hard code another full path instead

I agree that letting windows handle WSL path is the best course of action.

~~But apparently when people update, WSL leaves certain traces behind which can conflict on path. I think if people download from the store, and new installs of WSL using the store, will use the WindowsApps path, but non-store wsl distros and older installations will use the programfiles or system32 path. I honestly think system32 should be the default, but I see the issue here. I guess the change shouldn't be here but on machine init, if it fails because it does not have access to WindowsApps\wsl.exe it should try Program Files\wsl.exe and system32\wsl.exe later. Will revert these changes and try and implement that on machine init instead.~~

Just read Craig's post on the issue, apparently the default path for WSL is the programfiles one, but the path should set to system32 first. Just saw l0rd's comment also. My bad, busy day, missed that one. As Craig said the exe in appdata is merely an app execution alias (which might explain why we cannot directly access it sometimes).

Should we remove and just find from PATH or try system32 and then programfiles? What do you guys think?

> Should we remove and just find from PATH or try system32 and then programfiles? What do you guys think?

Yes, that makes sense. Thank you @mvfc

> > Should we remove and just find from PATH or try system32 and then programfiles? What do you guys think?
> 
> Yes, that makes sense. Thank you @mvfc

I mean, just get the default one, the first in the PATH.
@l0rd after much trial and error on my part and getting the ci to work locally, i think i got it :-) tested and it worked on my machine plus tests are passing
@mvfc I will review it tomorrow. Can you please squash the commits and force push?

Thank you @mvfc. Regarding @Luap99's concern that different WSL clients potentially view a different set of VMs, this is not the case, and it's indeed reasonably safe to use the first client in `%PATH%`.

Anyway, the [build on macOS fails](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5579704247255040), but it doesn't seem related to this PR (cc @Luap99).

/approve
/lgtm
/hold

> Thank you @mvfc. Regarding @Luap99's concern that different WSL clients potentially view a different set of VMs, this is not the case, and it's indeed reasonably safe to use the first client in `%PATH%`.
> 
> Anyway, the [build on macOS fails](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5579704247255040), but it doesn't seem related to this PR (cc @Luap99).

yeah i did not change anything related to macOS from what I can see, and thanks for reviewing

> Anyway, the [build on macOS fails](https://cirrus-ci.com/task/5579704247255040), but it doesn't seem related to this PR (cc @Luap99).

Yeah that is the broken go 1.24.3 update where podman fails to build on macos. I thought I fixed the pool setup but it doesn't seem to work so I manually hotfixed them again and restarted.
A bit late to the party, but wanted to say thank you for this change! Should hopefully reduce some errors :)
